reviews.summary: This paper proposes to assign each node with a quasi-orthogonal (QO) vector as its id (signature) and then run pure message-passing over them, so that the obtained node embeddings can be used to estimate link-level structural features (e.g. common neighbors), which is unattainable by vanilla GNNs. A new framework based on estimated pairwise features is proposed for the link prediction task, whose effectiveness is evaluated on 13 non-attributed and attributed graphs.
reviews.strengths:
- Similarly to subgraph sketching, the author proposes an estimation-based approach to obtain pairwise structural features of link prediction heuristics under the message-passing framework. 
- The authors exploit the property of quasi-orthogonal (QO) vectors so that neighbor overlap-based heuristics other than CN can be estimated by the simplified MPNN framework.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
- In order to estimate pairwise features, the pure message passing adopted in MPLP loses the non-linearity of MPNN, which comprises the expressiveness of the framework.
- The adopted QO vector from DotHash enables more types of pairwise structural features (AA, RA), but it is still heuristic-based. Those features are empirically helpful for link prediction but also lose flexibility and capacity since they are fixed and predefined.
- The feature estimation of Eq. (5) still depends on the $r$-hop induced subgraph for estimating $r$-order intersection and difference of neighborhoods. Meanwhile, Theorem 3 shows that $\mathbf{h}_u^{r}$ and $\mathbf{h}_v^{r}$ can not be used for CN. This raises my concern over its scalability (feature propgation+subgraph extraction), and it would be great to see a clearer side-by-side runtime comparison with BUDDY [1], including preprocessing (node-wise) and estimating structural features (link-wise).

[1] Chamberlain, Benjamin Paul, et al. "Graph neural networks for link prediction with subgraph sketching." ICLR (2023).
reviews.questions:
- How would the sampling of neighborhoods in SAGE affect Thereom 1 and the estimator in Eq. (3)?
- What is the cost of generating QO vectors? Is there a principal way to pick its dimension? Will it scale to graphs larger than collab? How would the density of the graph affect its performance of runtime and estimation error?
- The order of features $r$ used in experiments is 2. Is it based on computation concerns? Can MPLP be applied beyond 2 hops?
- Sec 4.3 mentioned that Eq. (7) can be used for estimating triangles. Can MPLP be used for the triangle estimation task in Sec 6.1 of [2]?
- Is the $\text{GNN}(\cdot)$ in Eq. (4) and Eq. (8) referring to valina GNNs or the linearized version proposed in Eq. (3)?
- It would be great to provide more details regarding the baselines, especially differentiating ELPH and BUDDY [1], and NCN/NCNC-k [3] for interpreting the results of Table 2 and Fig 4.
- It is a standard procedure to remove target links in training for inductive settings. Similar approaches have been adopted in SEAL [4] and SUREL (mini-batch subgraph training) [5].

[2] Chen, Zhengdao, et al. "Can graph neural networks count substructures?." Advances in neural information processing systems 33 (2020): 10383-10395.   
[3] Wang, Xiyuan, Haotong Yang, and Muhan Zhang. "Neural Common Neighbor with Completion for Link Prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.00890 (2023).  
[4] Zhang, Muhan, and Yixin Chen. "Link prediction based on graph neural networks." Advances in neural information processing systems 31 (2018).  
[5] Yin, Haoteng, et al. "Algorithm and system co-design for efficient subgraph-based graph representation learning." VLDB (2022).
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

Summary:
This paper proposes to assign each node with a quasi-orthogonal (QO) vector as its id (signature) and then run pure message-passing over them, so that the obtained node embeddings can be used to estimate link-level structural features (e.g. common neighbors), which is unattainable by vanilla GNNs. A new framework based on estimated pairwise features is proposed for the link prediction task, whose effectiveness is evaluated on 13 non-attributed and attributed graphs.

Strengths:
- Similarly to subgraph sketching, the author proposes an estimation-based approach to obtain pairwise structural features of link prediction heuristics under the message-passing framework. 
- The authors exploit the property of quasi-orthogonal (QO) vectors so that neighbor overlap-based heuristics other than CN can be estimated by the simplified MPNN framework.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
- In order to estimate pairwise features, the pure message passing adopted in MPLP loses the non-linearity of MPNN, which comprises the expressiveness of the framework.
- The adopted QO vector from DotHash enables more types of pairwise structural features (AA, RA), but it is still heuristic-based. Those features are empirically helpful for link prediction but also lose flexibility and capacity since they are fixed and predefined.
- The feature estimation of Eq. (5) still depends on the $r$-hop induced subgraph for estimating $r$-order intersection and difference of neighborhoods. Meanwhile, Theorem 3 shows that $\mathbf{h}_u^{r}$ and $\mathbf{h}_v^{r}$ can not be used for CN. This raises my concern over its scalability (feature propgation+subgraph extraction), and it would be great to see a clearer side-by-side runtime comparison with BUDDY [1], including preprocessing (node-wise) and estimating structural features (link-wise).

[1] Chamberlain, Benjamin Paul, et al. "Graph neural networks for link prediction with subgraph sketching." ICLR (2023).

Questions:
- How would the sampling of neighborhoods in SAGE affect Thereom 1 and the estimator in Eq. (3)?
- What is the cost of generating QO vectors? Is there a principal way to pick its dimension? Will it scale to graphs larger than collab? How would the density of the graph affect its performance of runtime and estimation error?
- The order of features $r$ used in experiments is 2. Is it based on computation concerns? Can MPLP be applied beyond 2 hops?
- Sec 4.3 mentioned that Eq. (7) can be used for estimating triangles. Can MPLP be used for the triangle estimation task in Sec 6.1 of [2]?
- Is the $\text{GNN}(\cdot)$ in Eq. (4) and Eq. (8) referring to valina GNNs or the linearized version proposed in Eq. (3)?
- It would be great to provide more details regarding the baselines, especially differentiating ELPH and BUDDY [1], and NCN/NCNC-k [3] for interpreting the results of Table 2 and Fig 4.
- It is a standard procedure to remove target links in training for inductive settings. Similar approaches have been adopted in SEAL [4] and SUREL (mini-batch subgraph training) [5].

[2] Chen, Zhengdao, et al. "Can graph neural networks count substructures?." Advances in neural information processing systems 33 (2020): 10383-10395.   
[3] Wang, Xiyuan, Haotong Yang, and Muhan Zhang. "Neural Common Neighbor with Completion for Link Prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.00890 (2023).  
[4] Zhang, Muhan, and Yixin Chen. "Link prediction based on graph neural networks." Advances in neural information processing systems 31 (2018).  
[5] Yin, Haoteng, et al. "Algorithm and system co-design for efficient subgraph-based graph representation learning." VLDB (2022).

reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Summary:**
This manuscript introduces a novel approach that assigns each network node a quasi-orthogonal (QO) vector as its unique identifier or signature. Subsequently, it employs a pure message-passing mechanism over these vectors to derive node embeddings. These embeddings are then utilized to approximate link-level structural characteristics, such as common neighbors, which are typically challenging for conventional Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to capture. The paper proposes a new framework for link prediction, founded on these estimated pairwise features, and evaluates its efficacy across 13 distinct non-attributed and attributed graph datasets.

**Strengths:**
*   The authors present a novel estimation-driven methodology for acquiring pairwise structural properties, relevant to link prediction heuristics, within a message-passing framework. This approach shares conceptual similarities with subgraph sketching techniques.
*   The work effectively leverages the distinct properties of quasi-orthogonal (QO) vectors. This enables the estimation of various heuristics based on neighborhood overlap, extending beyond just common neighbors to include measures like Adamic-Adar (AA) and Resource Allocation (RA), all through a streamlined message-passing neural network (MPNN) structure.
*   The manuscript is presented with clarity and is readily comprehensible, making the proposed concepts accessible to readers.

**Weaknesses:**
*   A notable limitation of the proposed MPLP framework is that the pure message-passing scheme, adopted for feature estimation, sacrifices the inherent non-linearity typically present in standard MPNNs. This simplification consequently diminishes the overall expressive power of the framework.
*   While the integration of QO vectors, borrowed from DotHash, facilitates the estimation of additional pairwise structural features (e.g., AA, RA), these remain fundamentally heuristic-driven. Their fixed and predefined nature introduces a constraint on flexibility and capacity, despite their empirical utility in link prediction tasks.
*   A significant concern arises regarding the scalability of the proposed method. The estimation of r-order neighborhood intersections and differences, as depicted in Equation (5), necessitates the extraction of r-hop induced subgraphs. Furthermore, Theorem 3 explicitly states that the learned representations $\mathbf{h}_u^{r}$ and $\mathbf{h}_v^{r}$ are unsuitable for direct Common Neighbor (CN) estimation. To address this, it would be highly beneficial to include a more explicit, side-by-side runtime comparison with methods like BUDDY [1], detailing both preprocessing steps (node-wise) and the time required for structural feature estimation (link-wise).

**Questions & Suggestions for Improvement:**
*   Could the authors elaborate on how the neighborhood sampling strategy employed in GNNs like GraphSAGE might influence the validity of Theorem 1 and the performance of the estimator presented in Equation (3)?
*   Further clarification is needed regarding the computational overhead associated with generating the QO vectors. Is there a principled methodology for determining their optimal dimension? How does this approach scale to substantially larger graphs than 'collab', and what is the impact of graph density on both runtime efficiency and estimation accuracy?
*   The experimental results primarily utilize a feature order 'r' of 2. Is this choice driven by computational considerations, and can the MPLP framework be effectively extended to higher hop counts?
*   Section 4.3 suggests that Equation (7) can be utilized for triangle estimation. Does the MPLP framework lend itself to addressing the specific triangle estimation task outlined in Section 6.1 of [2]?
*   Please clarify whether the $\text{GNN}(\cdot)$ notation in Equations (4) and (8) refers to conventional GNN architectures or specifically to the linearized version introduced in Equation (3).
*   To enhance the interpretability of results presented in Table 2 and Figure 4, it would be highly beneficial to provide more comprehensive details regarding the baseline methods, particularly distinguishing between ELPH and BUDDY [1], as well as NCN/NCNC-k [3].
*   For inductive settings, it is standard practice to exclude target links from the training data, a methodology observed in works such as SEAL [4] and SUREL (which employs mini-batch subgraph training) [5]. It would be important to confirm if this practice was consistently followed in the presented experiments.

**Rating:** 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: RINGER: Conformer Ensemble Generation of Macrocyclic Peptides with Sequence-Conditioned Internal Coordinate Diffusion

reviews.summary:
This paper addresses the challenge of generating macrocyclic peptides. The approach involves training a discrete-time diffusion model on the internal coordinates, which is implemented using a transformer equipped with its specific invariant cyclic positional encoding tailored for this generation task. During inference, the set of angles and torsions generated undergoes a refinement phase through constrained optimization.

The primary contribution of this work is the innovative architectural design tailored to tackle conformer generation for this particular class of molecules. Additionally, the paper provides comprehensive experimental evidence to establish the suitability of their approach for the problem.
reviews.strengths:
Significance:
Macrocyclic peptides represent a crucial category in therapeutics, and enhancing the precision and efficiency of conformer generation can profoundly impact drug discovery. Thus, this paper addresses a highly significant problem in the field.

Originality:
The paper introduces two key technical innovations. First, it adapts the positional encoding of the transformer architecture to better suit cyclic peptides, showcasing the authors' domain-specific knowledge in their modeling approach. Second, the paper presents a straightforward yet effective ring-closing procedure based on constraint optimization. Both solutions highlight that the authors intelligently use their task-specific insights. Moreover, the paper goes beyond traditional metrics for conformer generation, introducing novel evaluation criteria better aligned with the task.

Clarity and Quality:
The text is well-crafted, effectively motivating the problem, and the literature review is well-structured and relevant to the context.

Summary:
This paper addresses the challenge of generating macrocyclic peptides. The approach involves training a discrete-time diffusion model on the internal coordinates, which is implemented using a transformer equipped with its specific invariant cyclic positional encoding tailored for this generation task. During inference, the set of angles and torsions generated undergoes a refinement phase through constrained optimization.

The primary contribution of this work is the innovative architectural design tailored to tackle conformer generation for this particular class of molecules. Additionally, the paper provides comprehensive experimental evidence to establish the suitability of their approach for the problem.

Strengths:
Significance:
Macrocyclic peptides represent a crucial category in therapeutics, and enhancing the precision and efficiency of conformer generation can profoundly impact drug discovery. Thus, this paper addresses a highly significant problem in the field.

Originality:
The paper introduces two key technical innovations. First, it adapts the positional encoding of the transformer architecture to better suit cyclic peptides, showcasing the authors' domain-specific knowledge in their modeling approach. Second, the paper presents a straightforward yet effective ring-closing procedure based on constraint optimization. Both solutions highlight that the authors intelligently use their task-specific insights. Moreover, the paper goes beyond traditional metrics for conformer generation, introducing novel evaluation criteria better aligned with the task.

Clarity and Quality:
The text is well-crafted, effectively motivating the problem, and the literature review is well-structured and relevant to the context.

Weaknesses:
An ablation study is essential to elucidate the respective contributions of the positional encoder and the ring closing algorithm to the overall performance.

The benchmarking falls short in including some of the most recent diffusion models for small molecule conformer generation. While the authors acknowledge their limitations, substantiating these claims with experimental evidence is crucial. It would be valuable to include models such as TorsionDiff and a non-diffusion model like GFlowNets for a more comprehensive evaluation.

Questions:
- why macrocycles with fixed bond distances contain three redundant torsional angles and two redundant bond angles ?

- What is the information regarding the rejection rate for samples where the ring torsion fingerprint deviation exceeds 0.01 before and after optimization using Equation 3?

- What's the rationale for not directly modeling non-rotatable side-chain groups like phenyl rings and instead generating them using RDKit?

- Why was the training set restricted to 30 conformers per molecule with the lowest energy as opposed to a threshold based on the lowest energy?

- I'm seeking clarification on why, in section 4.4, you claim that the additional xTB optimization demonstrates the efficacy of the diffusion scheme in achieving diverse sampling. Could you elaborate on this point?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
This paper presents an innovative approach to the challenging problem of generating conformers for macrocyclic peptides. The authors propose a discrete-time diffusion model, trained on internal coordinates, which leverages a transformer architecture. A key feature of this architecture is its custom-designed invariant cyclic positional encoding, specifically tailored for this class of molecules. During the inference stage, the generated set of angles and torsions undergoes a refinement process using constrained optimization.

The primary strengths of this work lie in its novel architectural design, which is well-suited for macrocyclic conformer generation, and the robust experimental validation provided to demonstrate the efficacy of their method.

### Strengths:

*   **Significance:** Macrocyclic peptides hold significant therapeutic potential, making advancements in the precision and efficiency of their conformer generation critically important for drug discovery efforts. The problem addressed by this paper is therefore of substantial relevance to the field.
*   **Originality:** The paper introduces two notable technical contributions. First, the adaptation of the transformer's positional encoding to accommodate cyclic peptides demonstrates a deep understanding of domain-specific challenges and intelligent model design. Second, the development of a straightforward yet highly effective ring-closing procedure, implemented via constraint optimization, further underscores the authors' insightful application of task-specific knowledge. Beyond these architectural innovations, the paper also distinguishes itself by incorporating novel evaluation metrics that are more pertinent to the specific task of macrocycle conformer generation than traditional approaches.
*   **Clarity and Quality:** The manuscript is exceptionally well-written. The problem is clearly motivated, and the literature review is both comprehensive and directly relevant to the core subject matter.

### Weaknesses:

*   **Ablation Study:** To fully understand the individual contributions of the unique positional encoder and the ring-closing algorithm to the overall performance, an ablation study is essential. This would help delineate the impact of each component.
*   **Benchmarking Scope:** The current benchmarking could be expanded to include some of the more recent diffusion models designed for small molecule conformer generation. While the authors acknowledge certain limitations, empirical evidence supporting these claims through direct comparison is crucial. Incorporating models such as TorsionDiff, as well as a non-diffusion model like GFlowNets, would provide a more comprehensive and robust evaluation of the proposed method's standing in the current landscape.

### Questions for the Authors:

*   Could you elaborate on why macrocycles with fixed bond distances are considered to contain three redundant torsional angles and two redundant bond angles?
*   Please provide data on the rejection rate for samples where the ring torsion fingerprint deviation exceeds 0.01, both before and after the application of optimization using Equation 3.
*   What was the specific reasoning behind generating non-rotatable side-chain groups, such as phenyl rings, using RDKit rather than attempting to model them directly within your framework?
*   Why was the training set explicitly limited to 30 conformers per molecule with the lowest energy, as opposed to using an energy-based threshold for conformer inclusion?
*   In section 4.4, you claim that the additional xTB optimization demonstrates the efficacy of your diffusion scheme in achieving diverse sampling. Could you please provide further clarification or elaboration on this particular point?

### Rating:

**8: accept, good paper**

reviews.summary:
The authors present their method to generate ensembles of macrocycle conformer rings. Specifically, their model takes a 2D structure for a macrocycle peptide and generates 3D coordinates in the form of bond angle and torsional distributions. 

They test their model with and without side chains in both conditional and unconditional generation. Their method uses diffusion to generate its values and ultimately serves a purpose similar to alphafold; in that it predicts spatial characteristics of the structure from the composition of bonds and atoms in the base structure. 

In figure 2, the authors demonstrate that their method can estimate characteristics measured from test samples. In table 1, they show that their method is better able to estimate these values compared to existing methods.
reviews.strengths: The authors present a novel use of diffusion to generate macrocycle peptides. They show that their method can outperform existing methods by a significant margin and is able to produce estimates quite similar to test samples.
reviews.weaknesses:
The paper doesn’t focus on its implementation details as well as it could. Most of the necessary details are there, but it also isn’t clear that their method could be definitively replicated from the details given. A system diagram or some other flowchart outlining their method could help elevate the paper. 

The paper initially gave me the impression that the full structure was being generated until this was cleared up by figure 1. 

While this is not a reason to reject the paper, I believe the paper could flow better if it was immediately clear exactly what are the inputs and outputs to their method. Additionally, they should, either in the abstract or in the beginning of the methods section, state in plain language what challenges their method overcomes that previous methods were insufficient to achieve. The authors do state what their method is generating, but the language could be improved to make their motivations clearer.
reviews.questions: The paper left me with no outstanding questions beyond certain small details which are not strictly necessary for understanding their method.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:
The authors present their method to generate ensembles of macrocycle conformer rings. Specifically, their model takes a 2D structure for a macrocycle peptide and generates 3D coordinates in the form of bond angle and torsional distributions. 

They test their model with and without side chains in both conditional and unconditional generation. Their method uses diffusion to generate its values and ultimately serves a purpose similar to alphafold; in that it predicts spatial characteristics of the structure from the composition of bonds and atoms in the base structure. 

In figure 2, the authors demonstrate that their method can estimate characteristics measured from test samples. In table 1, they show that their method is better able to estimate these values compared to existing methods.

Strengths:
The authors present a novel use of diffusion to generate macrocycle peptides. They show that their method can outperform existing methods by a significant margin and is able to produce estimates quite similar to test samples.

Weaknesses:
The paper doesn’t focus on its implementation details as well as it could. Most of the necessary details are there, but it also isn’t clear that their method could be definitively replicated from the details given. A system diagram or some other flowchart outlining their method could help elevate the paper. 

The paper initially gave me the impression that the full structure was being generated until this was cleared up by figure 1. 

While this is not a reason to reject the paper, I believe the paper could flow better if it was immediately clear exactly what are the inputs and outputs to their method. Additionally, they should, either in the abstract or in the beginning of the methods section, state in plain language what challenges their method overcomes that previous methods were insufficient to achieve. The authors do state what their method is generating, but the language could be improved to make their motivations clearer.

Questions:
The paper left me with no outstanding questions beyond certain small details which are not strictly necessary for understanding their method.

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This manuscript introduces a novel computational approach for generating ensembles of macrocycle conformer rings. The proposed model takes a 2D structural representation of a macrocyclic peptide as input and predicts its 3D coordinates, specifically in the form of bond angle and torsional distributions. The authors leverage a diffusion-based generative process for these values, serving a purpose analogous to systems like AlphaFold in its aim to infer the spatial characteristics of a structure from its underlying atomic and bond composition. The model's efficacy is evaluated under various conditions, including the presence and absence of side chains, and through both conditional and unconditional generation scenarios. Empirical results presented in Figure 2 indicate the method's capability to accurately estimate characteristics derived from test samples, while Table 1 demonstrates its superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methodologies for these estimations.

**Strengths:**
The authors present an innovative application of diffusion models for the generation of macrocycle peptide conformers. A significant strength of this work is its demonstrated ability to substantially outperform current methods, achieving a remarkable degree of accuracy where its estimates closely align with those observed in actual test samples.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Replicability and Implementation Clarity:** While the foundational details are largely present, the paper's focus on granular implementation specifics could be improved. It is not entirely clear whether another researcher could definitively replicate the method solely based on the information provided. Integrating a comprehensive architectural diagram or a process flowchart would significantly enhance the clarity and replicability of the proposed methodology.
2.  **Initial Scope Ambiguity:** The initial presentation of the method's scope was somewhat ambiguous, giving the impression that full 3D structures were being generated, a point only clarified upon reviewing Figure 1. To improve the paper's flow and immediate comprehension, it would be beneficial to explicitly and clearly state the precise inputs and outputs of the method at the outset.
3.  **Motivation and Problem Statement:** The authors articulate what their method generates, but the underlying motivations and the specific challenges it addresses could be more explicitly highlighted. It would greatly benefit the reader if the paper, either in the abstract or early in the methods section, articulated in plain language the limitations of previous approaches that this novel method successfully overcomes. Enhancing this language would provide a clearer understanding of the work's significance and its contribution to the field.

**Questions:**
The paper is generally well-understood, leaving no major outstanding questions beyond a few minor technical details that do not impede the overall comprehension of the method.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces RINGER, a novel solution for generating conformations of macrocycle peptides. RINGER is a diffusion-based model with a Transformer as its core architecture. To maintain SE(3)-invariance, RINGER operates on torsion and bond angles, and the ultimate coordinates are generated through a post hoc optimization process. RINGER is capable of performing both backbone (unconditional) generation and macrocycle (conditional) generation, and extensive experiments validate its effectiveness.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper delves into a relatively underexplored research area-conformation generation for macrocycle peptides. The proposed method, RINGER, has demonstrated commendable results in terms of both quality and efficiency, achieving satisfying outcomes in a mere 20 steps.
2. The paper conducted a wide array of experiments, providing robust evidence to substantiate the effectiveness of RINGER.
3. This paper takes into account the cyclic symmetry inherent to macrocycles and devises a novel relative positional encoding method that effectively incorporates this unique property.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. I do not think novelty is enough. RINGER shares similarities with FoldingDiff [1]. The differentiating factor lies in RINGER's introduction of a unique relative positional encoding, specifically designed to account for cyclic symmetry. It's important to note that there appears to be a lack of an ablation study on this proposed positional encoding, which could provide valuable insights.
2. Additionally, from a machine learning perspective, one may question the inherent challenges of conformation generation for macrocyclic peptides. It might be worthwhile to explore whether adapting methods from other molecule types is a feasible approach, as machine learning methods may not be strongly influenced by molecular variations.
3. In the context of unconditional generation tasks, a comparison with other existing methods would be highly valuable in order to assess RINGER's performance and capabilities more comprehensively. Additionally, in the comparison of conditional generation, where rRMSD and rTFD metrics are employed, it appears that there is a absence of a method focused on backbone generation.

[1] Wu K E, Yang K K, Berg R, et al. Protein structure generation via folding diffusion. arXiv preprint arXiv:2209.15611, 2022.
reviews.questions:
1. Why the baseline excludes Torsional Diffusion [2]? I can understand that ‘Methods such as torsional diffusion only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design.’, but I think Torsional Diffusion can be compared ‘in the context of all-atom geometries (RMSD)’ in section 4.3 if I do not misunderstand.
2. Why GeoDiff-Macro performs so poor? Can you provide experiment details of GeoDiff-Macro?

[2] Jing B, Corso G, Chang J, et al. Torsional diffusion for molecular conformer generation. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2022, 35: 24240-24253.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper introduces RINGER, a novel solution for generating conformations of macrocycle peptides. RINGER is a diffusion-based model with a Transformer as its core architecture. To maintain SE(3)-invariance, RINGER operates on torsion and bond angles, and the ultimate coordinates are generated through a post hoc optimization process. RINGER is capable of performing both backbone (unconditional) generation and macrocycle (conditional) generation, and extensive experiments validate its effectiveness.

Strengths:
1. This paper delves into a relatively underexplored research area-conformation generation for macrocycle peptides. The proposed method, RINGER, has demonstrated commendable results in terms of both quality and efficiency, achieving satisfying outcomes in a mere 20 steps.
2. The paper conducted a wide array of experiments, providing robust evidence to substantiate the effectiveness of RINGER.
3. This paper takes into account the cyclic symmetry inherent to macrocycles and devises a novel relative positional encoding method that effectively incorporates this unique property.

Weaknesses:
1. I do not think novelty is enough. RINGER shares similarities with FoldingDiff [1]. The differentiating factor lies in RINGER's introduction of a unique relative positional encoding, specifically designed to account for cyclic symmetry. It's important to note that there appears to be a lack of an ablation study on this proposed positional encoding, which could provide valuable insights.
2. Additionally, from a machine learning perspective, one may question the inherent challenges of conformation generation for macrocyclic peptides. It might be worthwhile to explore whether adapting methods from other molecule types is a feasible approach, as machine learning methods may not be strongly influenced by molecular variations.
3. In the context of unconditional generation tasks, a comparison with other existing methods would be highly valuable in order to assess RINGER's performance and capabilities more comprehensively. Additionally, in the comparison of conditional generation, where rRMSD and rTFD metrics are employed, it appears that there is a absence of a method focused on backbone generation.

[1] Wu K E, Yang K K, Berg R, et al. Protein structure generation via folding diffusion. arXiv preprint arXiv:2209.15611, 2022.

Questions:
1. Why the baseline excludes Torsional Diffusion [2]? I can understand that ‘Methods such as torsional diffusion only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design.’, but I think Torsional Diffusion can be compared ‘in the context of all-atom geometries (RMSD)’ in section 4.3 if I do not misunderstand.
2. Why GeoDiff-Macro performs so poor? Can you provide experiment details of GeoDiff-Macro?

[2] Jing B, Corso G, Chang J, et al. Torsional diffusion for molecular conformer generation. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2022, 35: 24240-24253.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission introduces RINGER, an innovative diffusion-based model designed for the generation of macrocycle peptide conformations. RINGER leverages a Transformer architecture and ensures SE(3)-invariance by operating on internal coordinates (torsion and bond angles), with final Cartesian coordinates derived through a subsequent optimization step. The model demonstrates versatility, capable of both unconditional (backbone) and conditional (macrocycle) generation tasks, supported by extensive experimental validation.

**Strengths:**
1.  The paper addresses a challenging and relatively underexplored niche within computational biology: the conformational generation of macrocyclic peptides. RINGER presents a highly effective solution, exhibiting commendable performance in terms of both output quality and computational efficiency, achieving satisfactory results within a remarkably low number of diffusion steps (20 steps).
2.  The authors have conducted a comprehensive suite of experiments, providing robust empirical evidence that convincingly supports the efficacy and capabilities of the proposed RINGER model.
3.  A notable strength is the paper's ingenious approach to handling the inherent cyclic symmetry of macrocycles. This is achieved through the development of a novel relative positional encoding scheme, which effectively integrates this unique structural property into the model's architecture.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  A primary concern revolves around the degree of novelty. While RINGER introduces a specific relative positional encoding for cyclic symmetry, its core architectural foundation bears a strong resemblance to existing works such as FoldingDiff [1]. The distinctive positional encoding is highlighted as a key innovation, yet its true contribution remains somewhat unquantified due to the absence of an ablation study specifically investigating its impact. Such a study would be crucial for substantiating its value.
2.  From a broader machine learning perspective, the paper could benefit from a deeper exploration into the fundamental challenges specific to macrocyclic peptide conformation generation. It would be valuable to discuss whether methodologies developed for other molecular types could be adapted effectively, as the underlying machine learning principles might not be drastically different across various molecular structures. This would help contextualize the necessity for a macrocycle-specific approach.
3.  The comparative analysis could be significantly enhanced. For unconditional generation tasks, a more extensive comparison against a wider range of existing methods would provide a more complete picture of RINGER's performance relative to the state of the art. Furthermore, in the conditional generation experiments, particularly when evaluating with rRMSD and rTFD metrics, there appears to be a notable omission of a baseline method specifically focused on backbone generation.

**Questions:**
1.  Could the authors clarify the decision to exclude Torsional Diffusion [2] from the baselines? While the paper states that such methods "only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design," it seems plausible that Torsional Diffusion could still offer a relevant comparison "in the context of all-atom geometries (RMSD)" as discussed in Section 4.3, if my understanding is correct.
2.  The reported performance of GeoDiff-Macro appears significantly poor. Could the authors provide a more detailed explanation for this outcome and elaborate on the specific experimental setup and parameters used for GeoDiff-Macro?

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the conformer generation problem in molecular machine learning. Specifically, conformer generation for ring systems is challenging for previous approaches. This paper proposes a diffusion model over internal coordinates to generate macrocycle peptide conformers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approaches over previous methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper does capture an important problem --- sampling the conformational ensembles for structures with diverse ring systems and previous effort in this direction is relatively limited.
2. This paper proposes to use a diffusion model over internal coordinates (angles and dihedrals) is technically sound and efficient to reduce the degree of freedom (e.g. distances).
3. The empirical performance of the proposed method is excellent compared to the baseline methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
The technical contribution of this paper is limited (to the machine learning community), the way to build a diffusion model over angle and torsion space has been widely studied in the related literature. IMHO, the most interesting part of the paper is about how to capture ring system conformational changes with angles and dihedrals, however, it is discussed only very briefly in Sec 3.3. How do you determine the 3 torsional angles and 2 bond angles for a macrocycle (how many atoms are in the cycle? How about a two-ring system?) The post-processing optimization step seems an effective and efficient solution to reconstruct the cartesian coordinates for the macrocycles, but how do you assemble them back into the structure (assuming you are only optimizing for the rings)?

Overall, I think this is an interesting application paper to establish diffusion models to sample conformational changes for molecular structures, especially ring systems. Given it's an application paper, I would expect more discussions from the problem formulation side and why it should be designed in this way with more case studies to demonstrate, e.g. it could handle multiple ring systems. The critical part missing is how to extract the angles and dihedrals from the ring.
reviews.questions: See weaknesses.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the conformer generation problem in molecular machine learning. Specifically, conformer generation for ring systems is challenging for previous approaches. This paper proposes a diffusion model over internal coordinates to generate macrocycle peptide conformers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approaches over previous methods.

Strengths:
1. This paper does capture an important problem --- sampling the conformational ensembles for structures with diverse ring systems and previous effort in this direction is relatively limited.
2. This paper proposes to use a diffusion model over internal coordinates (angles and dihedrals) is technically sound and efficient to reduce the degree of freedom (e.g. distances).
3. The empirical performance of the proposed method is excellent compared to the baseline methods.

Weaknesses:
The technical contribution of this paper is limited (to the machine learning community), the way to build a diffusion model over angle and torsion space has been widely studied in the related literature. IMHO, the most interesting part of the paper is about how to capture ring system conformational changes with angles and dihedrals, however, it is discussed only very briefly in Sec 3.3. How do you determine the 3 torsional angles and 2 bond angles for a macrocycle (how many atoms are in the cycle? How about a two-ring system?) The post-processing optimization step seems an effective and efficient solution to reconstruct the cartesian coordinates for the macrocycles, but how do you assemble them back into the structure (assuming you are only optimizing for the rings)?

Overall, I think this is an interesting application paper to establish diffusion models to sample conformational changes for molecular structures, especially ring systems. Given it's an application paper, I would expect more discussions from the problem formulation side and why it should be designed in this way with more case studies to demonstrate, e.g. it could handle multiple ring systems. The critical part missing is how to extract the angles and dihedrals from the ring.

Questions:
See weaknesses.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This manuscript addresses the challenging problem of conformer generation within molecular machine learning, with a specific focus on the often-difficult task of accurately sampling configurations for molecular ring systems, an area where existing methods frequently struggle. The authors introduce a novel approach utilizing a diffusion model that operates directly on internal coordinates (bond angles and dihedral angles) to generate conformers for macrocyclic peptides. The empirical evaluations presented in the paper convincingly demonstrate the superior performance of their proposed method when benchmarked against current state-of-the-art techniques.

**Strengths:**
1.  The paper successfully identifies and tackles a critical challenge: the accurate sampling of conformational ensembles for molecular structures containing various ring systems, an area where prior research has been relatively sparse.
2.  The methodological choice of employing a diffusion model within an internal coordinate space (specifically angles and dihedrals) is technically robust and offers a computationally efficient way to manage the degrees of freedom compared to Cartesian representations.
3.  The empirical results are highly compelling, demonstrating a significant improvement in performance over existing baseline methodologies.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  A primary concern lies in the perceived limited novelty of the core technical contribution within the broader machine learning context. The application of diffusion models to angle and torsion spaces has been extensively explored in relevant literature, suggesting that the foundational modeling approach might not represent a significant advancement for the ML community.
2.  While the paper's most intriguing aspect is undoubtedly its approach to modeling conformational changes in ring systems using angles and dihedrals, this crucial discussion is regrettably concise in Section 3.3. This section would benefit greatly from a more in-depth explanation.
3.  Several key details regarding the practical implementation for ring systems remain unclear. For instance, the methodology for determining the specific three torsional angles and two bond angles for a macrocycle is not elaborated. Clarification is needed on how the number of atoms in a cycle influences this process, and how the model handles more complex architectures, such as systems with two rings. Furthermore, while the post-processing optimization step appears effective for reconstructing Cartesian coordinates for macrocycles, the process of reassembling these optimized ring structures back into the complete molecular context, especially if optimization is limited to the rings, requires further elucidation.

**Overall:**
In summary, this work presents an engaging application of diffusion models to address the challenging problem of molecular conformational sampling, particularly for ring systems. However, despite its strengths, the paper falls short in providing sufficient depth on the problem's specific nuances and the underlying design rationale. For an application-focused contribution, a more comprehensive discussion of the problem formulation, detailed justification for architectural choices, and illustrative case studies—including examples of multi-ring systems—would significantly strengthen its impact. The absence of a clear explanation regarding the extraction of angles and dihedrals directly from the ring structures represents a fundamental gap that needs to be addressed.

**Questions:**
Please refer to the 'Weaknesses' section for specific questions requiring clarification.

**Rating:**
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the conformer generation problem in molecular machine learning. Specifically, conformer generation for ring systems is challenging for previous approaches. This paper proposes a diffusion model over internal coordinates to generate macrocycle peptide conformers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approaches over previous methods.

Strengths:
1. This paper does capture an important problem --- sampling the conformational ensembles for structures with diverse ring systems and previous effort in this direction is relatively limited.
2. This paper proposes to use a diffusion model over internal coordinates (angles and dihedrals) is technically sound and efficient to reduce the degree of freedom (e.g. distances).
3. The empirical performance of the proposed method is excellent compared to the baseline methods.

Weaknesses:
The technical contribution of this paper is limited (to the machine learning community), the way to build a diffusion model over angle and torsion space has been widely studied in the related literature. IMHO, the most interesting part of the paper is about how to capture ring system conformational changes with angles and dihedrals, however, it is discussed only very briefly in Sec 3.3. How do you determine the 3 torsional angles and 2 bond angles for a macrocycle (how many atoms are in the cycle? How about a two-ring system?) The post-processing optimization step seems an effective and efficient solution to reconstruct the cartesian coordinates for the macrocycles, but how do you assemble them back into the structure (assuming you are only optimizing for the rings)?

Overall, I think this is an interesting application paper to establish diffusion models to sample conformational changes for molecular structures, especially ring systems. Given it's an application paper, I would expect more discussions from the problem formulation side and why it should be designed in this way with more case studies to demonstrate, e.g. it could handle multiple ring systems. The critical part missing is how to extract the angles and dihedrals from the ring.

Questions:
See weaknesses.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
